[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Geodetic+Reference+System+1980%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Geodetic+Reference+System+1980%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Geodetic+Reference+System+1980%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Geodetic+Reference+System+1980%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(February 2009)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

(**GRS80**) consists of a global [reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid") and a [normal gravity](/wiki/Normal_gravity "Normal gravity") model.[[1]](#cite_note-Moritz1980-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Moritz1992-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Moritz2000-3) The GRS80 gravity model has been followed by the newer more accurate [Earth Gravitational Models](/wiki/Earth_Gravitational_Model "Earth Gravitational Model"), but the **GRS80 reference ellipsoid** is still the most accurate in use for [coordinate reference systems](/wiki/Coordinate_reference_system "Coordinate reference system"), e.g. for the international [ITRS](/wiki/International_Terrestrial_Reference_System_and_Frame "International Terrestrial Reference System and Frame"), the European [ETRS89](/wiki/European_Terrestrial_Reference_System_1989 "European Terrestrial Reference System 1989") and (with a 0,1 mm rounding error) for [WGS 84](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System") used for the American [Global Navigation Satellite System](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") ([GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System")).

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Geodesy "Template:Geodesy") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Geodesy "Template talk:Geodesy") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Geodesy "Special:EditPage/Template:Geodesy") |

The **Geodetic Reference System 1980** (**GRS80**) consists of a global [reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid") and a [normal gravity](/wiki/Normal_gravity "Normal gravity") model.[[1]](#cite_note-Moritz1980-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Moritz1992-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Moritz2000-3) The GRS80 gravity model has been followed by the newer more accurate [Earth Gravitational Models](/wiki/Earth_Gravitational_Model "Earth Gravitational Model"), but the **GRS80 reference ellipsoid** is still the most accurate in use for [coordinate reference systems](/wiki/Coordinate_reference_system "Coordinate reference system"), e.g. for the international [ITRS](/wiki/International_Terrestrial_Reference_System_and_Frame "International Terrestrial Reference System and Frame"), the European [ETRS89](/wiki/European_Terrestrial_Reference_System_1989 "European Terrestrial Reference System 1989") and (with a 0,1 mm rounding error) for [WGS 84](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System") used for the American [Global Navigation Satellite System](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") ([GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System")).

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_Reference_System_1980&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

[Geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") is the scientific discipline that deals with the measurement and representation of the [earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"), its [gravitational](/wiki/Gravitation "Gravitation") field and geodynamic phenomena ([polar motion](/wiki/Polar_motion "Polar motion"), earth [tides](/wiki/Tide "Tide"), and crustal motion) in three-dimensional, time-varying space.

The [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid") is essentially the figure of the Earth abstracted from its topographic features. It is an idealized equilibrium surface of sea water, the mean sea level surface in the absence of currents, air pressure variations etc. and continued under the continental masses. The geoid, unlike the ellipsoid, is irregular and too complicated to serve as the computational surface on which to solve geometrical problems like point positioning. The geometrical separation between it and the reference ellipsoid is called the geoidal [undulation](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/undulation "wiktionary:undulation"), or more usually the geoid-ellipsoid separation, *N*. It varies globally between ±110 m.

A [reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid"), customarily chosen to be the same size (volume) as the geoid, is described by its semi-major axis (equatorial
radius) *a* and flattening *f*. The quantity *f* = (*a*−*b*)/*a*, where *b* is the semi-minor axis (polar radius), is a purely geometrical one. The mechanical ellipticity of the earth (dynamical flattening, symbol *J*2) is determined to high precision by observation of satellite orbit perturbations. Its relationship with the geometric flattening is indirect. The relationship depends on the internal density distribution.

The 1980 Geodetic Reference System (GRS 80) posited a 6378137 m semi-major axis and a 1⁄298.257222101 flattening. This system was adopted at the XVII General Assembly of the International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics ([IUGG](/wiki/IUGG "IUGG")) in Canberra, Australia, 1979.

The GRS 80 reference system was originally used by the [World Geodetic System 1984](/wiki/WGS_84 "WGS 84") (WGS 84). The reference ellipsoid of WGS 84 now differs slightly due to later refinements.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The numerous other systems which have been used by diverse countries for their maps and charts are gradually dropping out of use as more and more countries move to global, geocentric reference systems using the GRS80 reference ellipsoid.

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_Reference_System_1980&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Definition")]

The reference ellipsoid is usually defined by its [semi-major axis](/wiki/Semi-major_axis "Semi-major axis") (equatorial
radius) ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc) and either its [semi-minor axis](/wiki/Semi-minor_axis "Semi-minor axis") (polar radius) ![{\displaystyle b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f11423fbb2e967f986e36804a8ae4271734917c3), [aspect ratio](/wiki/Aspect_ratio "Aspect ratio") ![{\displaystyle (b/a)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2983db16441b48aa0b728f1cab7514acf6fec8d3) or [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") ![{\displaystyle f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/132e57acb643253e7810ee9702d9581f159a1c61), but GRS80 is an exception: *four* independent constants are required for a complete definition. GRS80 chooses as these ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc), ![{\displaystyle GM}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d2d9b5427dc6e6f3c11f13d88a5b637b4bc24585), ![{\displaystyle J_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0f9986a8fbfd51097a5ff5e82d3252c9572b5835) and ![{\displaystyle \omega }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/48eff443f9de7a985bb94ca3bde20813ea737be8), making the geometrical constant ![{\displaystyle f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/132e57acb643253e7810ee9702d9581f159a1c61) a derived quantity.

Defining [geometrical](/wiki/Geometrical "Geometrical") [constants](/wiki/Constant_(mathematics) "Constant (mathematics)")
:   Semi-major axis = Equatorial Radius = ![{\displaystyle a=6\,378\,137\,\mathrm {m} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8760a3fbffa309ed6e5d3c628abd68104499816a);

Defining [physical](/wiki/Nature "Nature") [constants](/wiki/Constant_(mathematics) "Constant (mathematics)")
:   [Geocentric gravitational constant](/wiki/Geocentric_gravitational_constant "Geocentric gravitational constant") determined from the [gravitational constant](/wiki/Gravitational_constant "Gravitational constant") and the [earth mass](/wiki/Earth_mass "Earth mass") with atmosphere ![{\displaystyle GM=3986005\times 10^{8}\,\mathrm {m^{3}/s^{2}} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9322da6446dc48bb5c0b693e0ceb3ff6892bcf6d);
:   Dynamical form factor ![{\displaystyle J_{2}=108\,263\times 10^{-8}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d72ad089763758fac52d3b2fad355d5f43aaa194);
:   Angular velocity of rotation ![{\displaystyle \omega =7\,292\,115\times 10^{-11}\,\mathrm {s^{-1}} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d3046c48357e6626db9c8fc2dbb1df9c3902427e);

## Derived quantities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geodetic_Reference_System_1980&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Derived quantities")]

Derived geometrical constants (all rounded)
:   Flattening = ![{\displaystyle f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/132e57acb643253e7810ee9702d9581f159a1c61) = 0.003 352 810 681 183 637 418;
:   Reciprocal of flattening = ![{\displaystyle 1/f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e9cd0e60c02ddf533da2abed825389fe5a94b7d0) = 298.257 222 100 882 711 243;
:   Semi-minor axis = Polar Radius = ![{\displaystyle b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f11423fbb2e967f986e36804a8ae4271734917c3) = 6 356 752.314 140 347 m;
:   Aspect ratio = ![{\displaystyle b/a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0e0082708c4a962619f1b493a22dc776b5fbab62) = 0.996 647 189 318 816 363;
:   [Mean radius](/wiki/Earth_radius#Mean_radius:_R1 "Earth radius") as defined by the [International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics](/wiki/International_Union_of_Geodesy_and_Geophysics "International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics") (IUGG): ![{\displaystyle R_{1}=(2a+b)/3}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dd0e7b4af4e91123d962b74e42ce1ac836ccc938) = 6 371 008.7714 m;
:   [Authalic mean radius](/wiki/Earth_radius#Authalic_radius "Earth radius") = ![{\displaystyle R_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/35f571121c264178676d1df8ab899f238a39bc2c) = 6 371 007.1809 m;
:   Radius of a sphere of the same volume = ![{\displaystyle R_{3}=(a^{2}b)^{1/3}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e6867aa92ab728e101285a26cd66cf96a1e3f4df) = 6 371 000.7900 m;
:   Linear eccentricity = ![{\displaystyle c={\sqrt {a^{2}-b^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/78a9d2e194c630274028a18c59e4289981aad14d) = 521 854.0097 m;
:   [Eccentricity](/wiki/Eccentricity_(mathematics) "Eccentricity (mathematics)") of elliptical section through poles = ![{\displaystyle e={\frac {\sqrt {a^{2}-b^{2}}}{a}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9e547110997537f86ccb70c751df83b3b0959979) = 0.081 819 191 0428;
:   Polar radius of curvature = ![{\displaystyle a^{2}/b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/97fcf4793890bb6162d22bc30ce3e3efe05ef407) = 6 399 593.6259 m;
:   Equatorial radius of curvature for a meridian = ![{\displaystyle b^{2}/a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d7d021205fa73cc0e482b5ea56e635efc7573670) = 6 335 439.3271 m;
:   Meridian quadrant = 10 001 965.7292 m;

Derived physical constants (rounded)
:   Period of rotation ([sidereal day](/wiki/Sidereal_day "Sidereal day")) = ![{\displaystyle 2\pi /\omega }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7d0bc1e95b72696502acf4d7c3116fb6d4918167) = 86 164.100 637 s

The formula giving the eccentricity of the GRS80 spheroid is:[[1]](#cite_note-Moritz1980-1)

:   ![{\displaystyle e^{2}={\frac {a^{2}-b^{2}}{a^{2}}}=3J_{2}+{\frac {4}{15}}{\frac {\omega ^{2}a^{3}}{GM}}{\frac {e^{3}}{2q_{0}}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ec9228a5fe7dd71621db58474d33b2a19e6a6ea9)

where

:   ![{\displaystyle 2q_{0}=\left(1+{\frac {3}{e'^{2}}}\right)\arctan e'-{\frac {3}{e'}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f05d5750e444804439cdd09fed99ca138cd6112e)

and ![{\displaystyle e'={\frac {e}{\sqrt {1-e^{2}}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/36444e3fa774e4603d205d20bdde6598db5ef3dc) (so ![{\displaystyle \arctan e'=\arcsin e}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/74e3805a1887326870253c43c8d76d0111fbd02b)). The equation is solved iteratively to give

:   ![{\displaystyle e^{2}=0.00669\,43800\,22903\,41574\,95749\,48586\,28930\,62124\,43890\,\ldots }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7cc86fc0449d56ac572ea6abb378449ea4cc566b)

which gives

:   ![{\displaystyle f=1/298.25722\,21008\,82711\,24316\,28366\,\ldots .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ea09319343f9ed2c6a6619b426ba47a86bebc01d)
